posts.title: Welcome to ENNA: Why We Built an Open-Source Security Tool Index
posts.author: ENNA
posts.excerpt: ENNA launched today with 213 tools across 14 categories. Here's why we built it, what it covers, and where it's going.
posts.content:
Today we're launching ENNA, a curated index of open-source OSINT and reconnaissance tools built for security professionals, researchers, and anyone who works at the intersection of offensive security, blockchain forensics, and digital investigation.

## The Problem

If you've spent any time in security, you know the tooling landscape is enormous and fragmented. There are thousands of open-source tools scattered across GitHub, some actively maintained, some abandoned years ago. Finding the right tool for a specific job means searching through outdated blog posts, scrolling Reddit threads, or asking around in Discord servers. GitHub's own search is noisy. Awesome-lists help but give you a flat list of names with one-line descriptions and no way to filter, compare, or assess whether a tool is still alive.

We wanted something better. Not another awesome-list, but a proper index with real data.

## What ENNA Does

ENNA launched today with 213 tools across 14 categories:

- **Network Recon** - Port scanners, host discovery, network mapping (Nmap, Masscan, RustScan, Shodan CLI)
- **Subdomain Enumeration** - DNS discovery and asset mapping (Subfinder, Amass, Findomain)
- **Web Scanning** - Directory bruting, tech fingerprinting, vulnerability detection (Nuclei, Feroxbuster, ffuf, sqlmap)
- **OSINT Social** - Username lookup, profile analysis, social network mapping (Sherlock, Maigret)
- **OSINT General** - Email, phone, people search, metadata extraction (theHarvester, SpiderFoot, Holehe)
- **Vulnerability Scanning** - CVE scanning, misconfiguration detection (Nuclei, OpenVAS, Trivy)
- **Wireless** - WiFi auditing, Bluetooth, RF analysis (Aircrack-ng, Bettercap, Kismet)
- **Digital Forensics** - Disk forensics, memory analysis, file carving (Volatility, Autopsy, Velociraptor)
- **Crypto Tracing** - Blockchain analysis, wallet tracing, transaction mapping (Bitcoin Core CLI, Geth, Cast)
- **Password Attacks** - Hash cracking, brute force, credential tools (Hashcat, John the Ripper, Hydra)
- **Exploitation** - Exploit frameworks, payload generation, post-exploitation (Metasploit, Sliver, Mythic)
- **Phishing Analysis** - Phishing kits, URL analysis, email header analysis (GoPhish, Evilginx2)
- **Dual Use** - Legitimate tools with offensive applications (Wireshark, Burp Suite, Ghidra, YARA)
- **Offensive Ops** - Red team tooling, C2 frameworks, lateral movement (CrackMapExec, BloodHound, Impacket)

Every tool has a detailed description, installation commands for multiple platforms, use cases, alternative tools, and live GitHub statistics including star count, fork count, last commit date, and open issues. The GitHub stats are baked into the site at build time so they load instantly without API calls.

## How It's Built

ENNA is a Next.js application deployed on Vercel. The tool data lives in a single JSON file that gets enriched with GitHub API data at build time. The site is fully static, fast, and works well on mobile. We use Tailwind CSS with a custom dark theme, and the entire codebase is open source on GitHub.

Key technical decisions:

- **Static generation** - Every tool page, category page, and comparison page is pre-rendered at build time. No server-side rendering, no loading spinners, no API calls on page load.
- **Baked GitHub stats** - Rather than hitting the GitHub API on every page load (and dealing with rate limits, loading states, and failures), we fetch stats once during build and embed them in the JSON. This means the site works perfectly even if GitHub is down.
- **Comparison pages** - Tools that list alternatives automatically generate comparison pages. If Tool A lists Tool B as an alternative, you get a `/compare/tool-a-vs-tool-b` page with a side-by-side breakdown.

## The Library

Beyond tools, ENNA includes a curated library of books covering penetration testing, OSINT, social engineering, blockchain, malware analysis, and more. Each book has multiple purchase format options. We also list recommended hardware for security research.

## What's Next

This is just the launch. We have plans to expand the tool database significantly, add more categories, improve search and filtering, and build out comparison features. We'll be publishing weekly updates every Monday covering new additions and changes.

If you have tool suggestions, corrections, or want to contribute, the project is open source at github.com/1oosedows/enna.

Welcome to ENNA.

posts.title: Weekly Update: 55 New Tools, 3 Categories, Gear Page, and 22 New Books
posts.author: ENNA
posts.excerpt: Our first weekly update covers a massive expansion: 55 new tools, 3 new categories, a dedicated Gear page, 22 new books, and cross-linking across all 306 tools.
posts.content:
This is our first weekly update since launching ENNA on March 30th. It's been a busy week.

## By the Numbers

- **Tools**: 251 to 306 (+55)
- **Categories**: 16 to 19 (+3)
- **Books**: 23 to 45 (+22)
- **Alternative cross-links**: 672 bidirectional links across all tools
- **Every tool and book now has a detailed long description**

## Three New Categories

We added three categories that were clearly missing from the original launch:

**Threat Intelligence** - Platforms for IOC management, incident response, and threat sharing. This includes MISP, OpenCTI, TheHive, Cortex, GRR Rapid Response, KAPE, osquery, Wazuh, and Sigma. These tools are the backbone of SOC and CSIRT operations, and they deserved their own category rather than being scattered across forensics and OSINT.

**Container Security** - Docker and Kubernetes security tools including Falco (runtime threat detection), kube-hunter (K8s pentesting), CDK (container escape toolkit), Deepce (Docker enumeration), Dockle (image linting), and Syft (SBOM generation). Containerized infrastructure is everywhere now and the security tooling around it has matured significantly.

**Reverse Engineering** - Binary analysis, disassembly, and decompilation tools. We added dnSpy, ILSpy, x64dbg, Detect It Easy, angr, RetDec, Rizin, Unicorn Engine, and Capstone. These complement the existing entries for Ghidra, Radare2, and Cutter in the dual-use category, and all are now cross-linked.

## 55 New Tools

Here's everything we added, organized by category:

**Cloud Recon** (4 new, 14 total): Cartography, Steampipe, cloud_enum, CloudFlair, WeirdAAL

**Threat Intelligence** (9 new): MISP, OpenCTI, TheHive, GRR Rapid Response, KAPE, Cortex, osquery, Wazuh, Sigma

**Reverse Engineering** (9 new): dnSpy, ILSpy, x64dbg, Detect It Easy, angr, RetDec, Rizin, Unicorn Engine, Capstone

**Container Security** (6 new): Falco, kube-hunter, CDK, Deepce, Dockle, Syft

**Network Recon** (4 new): Scapy, Zeek, Arkime, Suricata

**Web Scanning** (5 new): Interactsh, Aquatone, EyeWitness, CRLFuzz, Smuggler

**Wireless** (5 new): Reaver, Pixiewps, mdk4, hostapd-mana, WiFi-Pumpkin3

**Offensive Ops** (3 new): Penelope, PetitPotam, Whisker

**Password Attacks** (3 new): CUPP, Patator, Mentalist

**Forensics** (2 new): RegRipper, bulk_extractor

**Dual Use** (3 new): Notify, PDTM, CyberChef

**OSINT General** (1 new): Ignorant

Every new tool has a full long description, installation commands, use cases, alternatives, tags, and GitHub stats.

## 672 Cross-Links

We ran a comprehensive cross-linking pass across all 306 tools. Every alternative reference is now bidirectional (if Tool A lists Tool B, Tool B lists Tool A) and we added sensible cross-category links. For example, the new reverse engineering tools link to Ghidra and Radare2 in dual-use, the new forensics tools link to Velociraptor and KAPE, and the container security tools link to Trivy and Grype in vulnerability scanning.

## 22 New Books

The library grew from 23 to 45 books with detailed long descriptions for every entry:

- **The Hacker Playbook** series (Books 1-3) by Peter Kim
- **Operator's Handbook trilogy** - Red Team, Blue Team, and Purple Team editions by Joseph Haynes
- **Tribe of Hackers** series (4 books) by Marcus Carey and Jennifer Jin
- **Social Engineering: The Science of Human Hacking** by Christopher Hadnagy
- **Silence on the Wire** and **The Tangled Web** by Michal Zalewski
- **Sandworm** and **Tracers in the Dark** by Andy Greenberg
- **Countdown to Zero Day** by Kim Zetter
- **Dark Wire** by Joseph Cox
- **Mastering Ethereum** and **Mastering Bitcoin** by Andreas Antonopoulos
- **Operator Handbook** by Joshua Picolet
- **Red Team** by Micah Zenko

All existing books also received detailed long descriptions.

## New Gear Page

Hardware items have been split out of the Library into their own dedicated **Gear** page at `/gear`. Items are now organized by category: RF/SDR, Multi-Tools, RFID/NFC, WiFi Adapters, and Laptops. Each item has its own detail page at `/gear/[slug]` with proper breadcrumbs. All old `/library/` URLs for hardware items 301 redirect to their new `/gear/` locations.

## Other Improvements

- **All em-dashes removed** across the entire codebase, replaced with hyphens
- **Cover images** added for all 45 books via Open Library
- **Avatar images** added for all 306 tools (GitHub avatars for open-source tools, Google favicons for commercial tools)
- **robots.txt updated** to block crawlers from indexing font files and static JS bundles that were causing Google Search Console errors
- **301 redirects** added for all hardware URLs moving from `/library/` to `/gear/`

## What's Next

Next week we'll be looking at adding favorites/bookmarks, tag-based browsing, and expanding the comparison pages with side-by-side tables. If you have tool suggestions or feedback, the project is open source at github.com/1oosedows/enna.

See you next Monday.
